docs.title: Haies
docs.text: LSCE x LVMH Gaia project

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
First clone and cd into repository

git clone https://github.com/LSCE-forest/haies.git
cd haies

then install either using Docker or Conda as below

docs.title: Using Docker
docs.text:
Build the docker image

docker build -t haies -f Dockerfile.cuda .

get a shell to test

docker run -it \
    -v $(pwd):/app \
    -v ~/data:/data \
    haies bash

the first -v command mounts the haies code to the container (at /app) so that it works the same as an editable install. The second -v command mounts an arbitrary local directory containing data that you want to process. The data can be accessed from /data in your container.

docs.title: Using conda
docs.text:
For CPU-only PyTorch (works on macOS and Linux) for dev purposes only

conda env create -f env-cpu.yml
conda activate haies
pip install .

docs.title: Project: Haies
docs.text: The haies pipeline automates hedge segmentation and volume estimation from aerial imagery and LiDAR data. Detailed documentation is available in the docs/ directory (generate a webpage with uv run mkdocs serve).

docs.title: 0. Data Download
docs.text:
Download essential datasets from IGN (BD-Ortho, Lidar-HD, BD-Haie).

# Example: Download BD-Ortho and Lidar-HD
haies_download bdortho --output data/bdortho --department 17
haies_download lidar-hd --geojson-file aoi.geojson --output data/lidar --compress

docs.title: 1. Label Preprocessing
docs.text:
Generate 0-1 soft labels from BD Haie and LiDAR height models.

haies_segment_hedges --config-name hedges

docs.title: 2. Dataset Splitting
docs.text:
Divide the Area of Interest (AOI) into training, validation, and test sets.

haies_create_split --config-name create_split

docs.title: 3. Model Training
docs.text:
Train the multi-head model using a phased approach (pretraining on height, then full training).

# Phase 1: Pretraining
haies fit --config base.yaml --config pretrain.yaml

# Phase 2: Full Training
haies fit --config base.yaml --config train.yaml --model.init_args.net_ckpt_path="last.ckpt"

docs.title: 4. Inference
docs.text:
Run predictions over a new AOI to generate hedge maps and canopy height models.

haies predict --config base.yaml --config predict.yaml --model.init_args.net_ckpt_path="final.ckpt"

docs.title: 5. Indicators Computation
docs.text:
Compute physical indicators (biomass, volume) and track changes over time.

# Yearly indicators
haies_cover_stats --config-name indicators

# Difference between two years
haies_diff_stats --config-name diff_indicators

docs.title: Project: Vegetation monitoring between vines
docs.text: See INTERROW.md for more information.

docs.title: Google Drive folder download
docs.text:
To download publicly available Google Drive folders with resume capability:

export GDRIVE_API_KEY="your_api_key_here"
haies_gdrive_download --folder-id FOLDER_ID --output-dir /path/to/output

See Google Drive download documentation for setup instructions and additional options.

docs.title: MultiHeadDataModule
docs.text:
Bases: LightningDataModule

Datamodule for multi targets. See available backends in haies.data.config.

docs.title: overlapping_split(bbox, size, fraction_overlap=0)
docs.text:
Split a bounding box into equally sized possibly-overlapping sub-bounding boxes with side equal to size.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default bbox GeoBoundingBox required size float

The size of the sub-bounding boxes in the unit of the bbox.

required fraction_overlap float

Float ≥ 0 and < 1 giving the approximate area overlap between two neighbouring sub-bounding boxes. This is always a lower bound on the actual overlap.

0

Yields:

Type Description GeoBoundingBox

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If the bbox is smaller than the requested split size.

docs.title: Config dataclass
docs.text:
Typed view of configuration used by the datamodules.

Attributes:

Name Type Description geometries_path str | None

Path to the GeoDataFrame (Parquet or GeoJSON) containing tile geometries and raster paths. Can be None when running predict if predict_inputs_dir is given.

targets list[str]

List of target names to process.

target_scaling_factor dict[str, float] | None

Scaling factor applied to each target. Defaults to None.

replace_nan_by_zero_in_input bool

Replace NaNs in input features by zero. Defaults to False.

replace_nan_by_zero_in_target bool

Replace NaNs in target arrays by zero. Defaults to True.

target_band_list dict[str, list[int]] | None

Optional band selection for each target. Defaults to None.

patch_size int

Size of the tile patches in pixels. Defaults to 256.

batch_size int

Training batch size. Defaults to 32.

predict_patch_size int | None

Predict patch size. If None, uses patch_size. Defaults to None.

predict_batch_size int | None

Predict batch size. If None, uses batch_size. Defaults to None.

num_workers int

Number of workers for the dataloader. Defaults to 8.

persistent_workers bool

Whether to keep dataloader workers alive between epochs. Defaults to False.

normalization_constants dict[Literal['mean', 'std'], list[int]]

Mean and standard deviation values for normalization. Defaults to {'mean': [124, 124, 124], 'std': [124, 124, 124]}.

resolution float

Spatial resolution for raster reading. Defaults to 1.0.

target_max_value dict[str, float] | None

Maximum allowed values for targets. Defaults to None.

predict_inputs_dir str | None

An alternative way to read an AOI. Instead of reading the input image from a geometries.parquet, this uses the rasters in predict_inputs_dir that matches predict_glob_pattern. It is ignored if geometries_path is not None.

predict_dir str | None

The directory where predictions files are written to. Used to filter out the prediction dataloader to remove tiles for already existing predictions. If None, prediction tiles are not filtered and predictions are computed from scratch. Defaults to None. See also: sprout.callbacks.PredictionsToTifs.

predict_glob_pattern str | list[str]

One ore more glob pattern to filter image path names before predictions. Useful to run several prediction in parallel. An image path is kept if it matches any of the patterns. Can be used both with geometries_path and predict_inputs_dir. Defaults "*" which does not filter input paths.

docs.title: MultiHeadLoss
docs.text:
Bases: _Loss

Measures the element-wise mean square error, ignoring elements of target that have no data. Works with multiple prediction heads.

See :func:torch.nn.MSELoss for details.

docs.title: MetricsValuesConfig dataclass
docs.text:
The structured type for configuring info over a type of values (either labels or predictions).

Attributes:

Name Type Description year_to_path dict[int, Path]

Mapping from year to path to the tif/vrt/other values. Metrics are computed for each given year.

unit float | Unit

Unit in which values are expressed. A float is a factor by which the value is scaled, e.g. Unit.CENTIMETER is equivalent to unit = 100. Defaults to Unit.METER.

band int

Which band to read if the files are rasters. Defaults to using the first band.

column str

Which column to read if the files are dataframes. Defaults to using the column "target".

min_value float | None max_value float | None

docs.title: MetricsConfig dataclass
docs.text:
The structured type for configuring metric computations.

Attributes:

Name Type Description data_dir Path

Base directory. All other non-absolute paths are interpreted relative to data_dir.

pred MetricsValuesConfig

Configuration of prediction related info.

label MetricsValuesConfig

Configuration of label related info.

bins list[float]

List of separations of bins to compute per-bin metrics. For instance [0, 2, 5, 10] define the three bins [0-2], [2-5] and [5-10].

split_gdf_path Path

Path to a GeoDataFrame-readable file that describes the train/test/val splits. It must contain a "split" column which values are taken from the Split enum. Its geometry column describes the geometries of the train/test/val areas.

split_name Split

The choice of split over which to compute metrics.

folder_column_name str

The name of the column in split_gdf where the name of folder where to retrieve a geometry is stored (e.g., year, aoi, etc.)

task_type str

Either "regression" or "classification".

classification_threshold list[float]

Cut-off for 0/1 classification. Defaults to 0.5.

raster_mask dict[str, dict[int, str]] | None

Path to a raster file to optionaly further mask out the AOI (one per year) Defaults to None.

vector_mask_path Path | None

Path to a GeoDataFrame-readable file to optionaly further mask out the AOI using its geometry column. Defaults to None.

raster_mask_classes_to_keep list[int] | None

Classes to keep when masking with the raster mask

resolution (float, optional)

Resolution to up/down-sample to the labels and predictions. Defaults to 1.

resampling_method ResamplingMethod

Up/Down-sampling method. Defaults to ResamplingMethod.BILINEAR.

save_dir Path

Directory to write metrics and figures to. If None, uses data_dir / metrics. Defaults to None.

save_pred_fig bool

Whether to save some label/prediction scatter plots. Defaults to False.

metrics_kwargs dict | None

Optional kwargs for metrics computation.

how_to_combine_raster_and_vector_mask str

Defaults to "union"

discard_not_classified_labels bool

Defaults to True

save_fig_n_skip int

Defaults to 100

save_fig_vmax float

Defaults to 40

remove_h5 bool

Whether to remove the intermediary h5 files. Defaults to True.

docs.title: RegressionModule
docs.text:
Bases: LightningModule

Simple regression module.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default net Module

The backbone network.

required criterion _Loss

The loss for the training.

required optimizer OptimizerCallable | None

Optional optimizer. Defaults to None.

None lr_scheduler LRSchedulerCallable | None

Optional learning rate scheduler. Defaults to None.

None warmup_scheduler LRSchedulerCallable | None

Optional warmup scheduler. Defaults to None.

None optimizer_kwargs dict | None

Optional optimizer arguments. Defaults to None.

None predictions_save_dir str | Path | None

Optional directory to save predictions. Defaults to None.

None

Raises:

Type Description Exception

If optimizer_kwargs is provided.

docs.title: configure_optimizers()
docs.text: Read carefully Lightning CLI documentation on multiple schedulers. Note that it is possible to add multiple optimizers too if desired.

docs.title: MultiHeadModule
docs.text:
Bases: LightningModule

PyTorch Lightning module for multi-target learning with mixed regression and segmentation heads.

This module handles multiple prediction targets jointly, each with its own loss type (e.g. regression or segmentation). It supports separate activations per head, automated metric logging, and optional saving of predictions during inference.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default net Module

The neural network producing multiple output channels, one per target.

required criterion MultiHeadLoss

Composite loss function returning individual target losses and a total loss.

required target_types dict[str, Literal['regression', 'segmentation']]

Mapping of target names to task types. Defines how metrics are computed.

required optimizer OptimizerCallable

Callable returning a PyTorch optimizer instance.

required lr_scheduler list[LRSchedulerCallable] | LRSchedulerCallable | None

Callable returning a learning rate scheduler.

None warmup_scheduler LRSchedulerCallable | None

Callable returning a warmup scheduler applied per step.

None predictions_save_dir Path | None

Directory to save predictions and labels during predict_step.

None activation_pred dict[str, Module] | None

Optional activation functions applied to each target’s predictions after the forward pass.

None mask_threshold float

Threshold for converting probabilistic segmentation outputs into binary masks.

0.5 net_ckpt_path Path | None

Path to model weights. Defaults to None which keeps the model weighs as initialized.

None Notes
  • Regression metrics: MAE, MAPE, SMAPE (and masked variants).
  • Segmentation metrics: IoU, F1, precision, recall, accuracy.
  • NaN values in targets are ignored in metric computation.
  • Returns individual head predictions as {target_name: tensor} dictionaries.

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If the criterion targets do not match the module targets, or if there are unexpected keys in the checkpoint.

docs.title: configure_optimizers()
docs.text: Read carefully Lightning CLI documentation on multiple schedulers. Note that it is possible to add multiple optimizers too if desired.

Segmentation Network using timm models as backbone.

4 num_channels int

Number of input channels. Defaults to 1.

1 pretrained bool

Whether to use pretrained weights. Defaults to True.

True pretrained_path str | None

Path to the pretrained weights file. Defaults to None.

None img_size int

Size of the input image. Defaults to 512.

512 decoder_stride int

Stride used in the decoder. Defaults to 2.

2 n_heads int

Number of attention heads in the decoder. Defaults to 1.

1

docs.title: How to Use Haies
docs.text: This guide provides a step-by-step walkthrough for using haies to perform hedge segmentation and volume estimation. Whether you are reproducing existing experiments or applying the model to a new Area of Interest (AOI), follow these steps in order.

docs.title: Pipeline Overview
docs.text:
The haies pipeline consists of several stages:

  1. Environment Setup: Installing the package and configuring your working environment.

  2. Data Download: Fetching essential IGN datasets (BD-Ortho, Lidar-HD, BD-Haie).

  3. Label Preprocessing: Generating segmentation labels from raw vector data and LiDAR height models.

  4. Dataset Splitting: Organizing your data into training, validation, and test sets.

  5. Accelerating Training (Optional): Creating memory-mapped arrays for faster data access.

  6. Model Training: Training the multi-head deep learning model using a phased approach.

  7. Running Predictions: Using the trained model to generate hedge maps and height estimates.

  8. Computing Indicators: Calculating biomass, volume, and tracking changes over time.

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Multi-Head Learning: haies simultaneously predicts hedge presence (segmentation) and canopy height (regression).

  • Configuration-Driven: Most tools in haies use YAML configuration files (managed by Hydra or LightningCLI) to ensure reproducibility.

  • HPC Optimized: The pipeline is designed to run efficiently on high-performance computing clusters (like Jean Zay) but can also be used locally.

Ready to start? Begin with Environment Setup.

docs.title: Data Download
docs.text: The haies pipeline requires several datasets from the IGN (Institut national de l'information géographique et forestière) to train models and compute indicators. The haies_download CLI tool simplifies fetching these datasets.

docs.title: Available Datasets
docs.text: The CLI provides commands to download imagery, elevation, and vector data.

docs.title: 1. Aerial Imagery (BD Ortho)
docs.text:
Download RGB or IRC imagery for a specific year and department. This is the primary input for the model.

haies_download bdortho --output ./data/bdortho --year 2024 --department 17
  • --irc: Use this flag to download infrared imagery instead of RGB.
  • --in-parallel: Set to True (default) to download multi-part archives concurrently.

docs.title: 2. LiDAR Elevation (Lidar HD)
docs.text:
Download 1km x 1km LiDAR tiles (MNH - Modèle Numérique de Hauteur) that intersect a given Area of Interest (AOI).

haies_download lidar-hd --geojson-file ./aoi.geojson --output ./data/lidar --compress
  • --compress: Compresses the downloaded .tif files into .jp2 format to save space (recommended).

docs.title: 3. Hedge Database (BD Haie)
docs.text:
Download the national hedge database and convert it to a GeoParquet file. This is used as the ground truth for training targets.

haies_download bdhaie --output ./data/bdhaie.parquet

docs.title: 4. Forest Mask (Foret V1)
docs.text:
Download the national forest mask. This dataset is only used during the indicators computation step to ensure that only true hedges are analyzed, by filtering out large forested areas that might otherwise be misidentified.

haies_download foretv1 --output ./data/foret.parquet

docs.title: 5. Land Cover (OCS GE)
docs.text:
Download the Occupation du Sol à Grande Échelle (OCS GE) dataset for building masks.

haies_download ocs-ge --output ./data/ocsge.parquet --department 17 --year 2021

docs.title: Typical Workflow
docs.text:
To prepare data for training in a new department:

  1. Download imagery: haies_download bdortho --output data/bdortho --department 17
  2. Download LiDAR: haies_download lidar-hd --geojson-file aoi.geojson --output data/lidar --compress
  3. Download Ground Truth: haies_download bdhaie --output data/bdhaie.parquet
  4. Download Forest Mask (optional): haies_download foretv1 --output data/foret.parquet (required only for Indicators Computation).

Once downloaded, you can proceed to Label Preprocessing.

Next Step: Label Preprocessing

docs.title: Computing Indicators
docs.text: Once you have generated predictions for your Area of Interest, the final step is to compute physical indicators like biomass, volume, and tracking how they change over time. haies provides two tools for this: haies_cover_stats for yearly metrics and haies_diff_stats for comparing two different time periods.

docs.title: Configuration Details
docs.text:
Reference Configurations

Reference configurations for computing indicators are shown below. You can also find them in docs/example_configs/.

indicators.yamldiff_indicators.yaml 
data_path:
  height: "./outputs/predictions/height" # directory or .vrt
  segment: "./outputs/predictions/segment" # directory or .vrt

score_threshold: 20  # between 0 and 100

resolution: 0.2
scaling_factor: 100
buffer: 100

forest_mask_path: "./data/foret.parquet"
forest_buffer: 1 # meters
height_threshold: 0.8 # meters

save_dir: "./outputs/indicators"
grid_path: null  # if null, the whole data_path is used instead
split: null

linear:
  min_distance: 4
  sigma: 5
  l2_threshold: 0.1
  simplify_tolerance: 0.3 # meters

biomass:
  alpha: 0.1
  beta: 1.1

override: true

start_index: null
ix: 0
n_tiles_to_process: 20000

year1:
  data_path: null  # path to segmentation raster, or dict with 'segment' key
  hedges_linear_path: null  # path to the hedges_linear.parquet from indicators
  grid_path: null  # optional, inferred from data_path if None

year2:
  data_path: null  # path to segmentation raster, or dict with 'segment' key
  hedges_linear_path: null  # path to the hedges_linear.parquet from indicators
  grid_path: null  # optional, inferred from data_path if None

resolution: 0.2  # meters per pixel
tile_size: 5000  # meters
buffer: 0  # meters, buffer around tiles for edge handling (can be left 0)

diff:
  alpha: 30  # low threshold (segment score below this = no hedge)
  beta: 70   # high threshold (segment score above this = hedge)
  min_points: 10  # minimum sampled points meeting threshold
  min_length: 15  # meters, filter short hedges

save_dir: null  # required, where to write results
split: null  # optional filter on split column

docs.title: 1. Yearly Indicators
docs.text:
The haies_cover_stats tool computes hedge statistics for a single time period. It uses the predicted hedge segmentation and height models to estimate:

  • Length: Total linear length of hedges (computed via skeletonization).

  • Area: Total surface area of hedges.

  • Volume: Total volume based on the canopy height model.

  • Biomass: Estimated biomass using allometric equations.

Forest Filtering

To improve accuracy, the tool can use a forest mask (see Data Download) to filter out detections within large forested areas, ensuring that the computed indicators are specific to hedges.

docs.title: Running Yearly Indicators
docs.text:
# Compute indicators for a single year
haies_cover_stats --config-path <path_to_config_dir> --config-name indicators

docs.title: Output Artifacts
docs.text:
• grid_stats.parquet: A GeoDataFrame with count, length, and area stats per tile.

  • hedges_linear.parquet: Linear features (skeletons) extracted from the segmentation masks.

  • compute_cover_indicators_on_grid_config.yaml: The configuration used for the computation.

docs.title: 2. Tracking Changes (Difference Indicators)
docs.text: If you have predictions for two different years, you can use haies_diff_stats to track where hedges have appeared or disappeared. This is especially useful for monitoring carbon sequestration and hedge degradation.

docs.title: Running Difference Indicators
docs.text:
# Compute differences between two years
haies_diff_stats --config-path <path_to_config_dir> --config-name diff_indicators

docs.title: Configuration Details
docs.text:
The difference tool expects configurations for both year1 and year2:

year1:
  data_path: /path/to/predictions/year1/
  hedges_linear_path: /path/to/save/indicators/year1/hedges_linear.parquet

year2:
  data_path: /path/to/predictions/year2/
  hedges_linear_path: /path/to/save/indicators/year2/hedges_linear.parquet

# Save directory for difference results
save_dir: /path/to/save/diff_indicators

# Difference parameters (thresholds for appearance/disappearance)
diff:
  alpha: 0.3
  beta: 0.7
  min_points: 5
  min_length: 10

docs.title: Output Artifacts
docs.text:
• diff_stats.parquet: Statistics for appeared and disappeared hedges per tile.

  • appeared_hedges_linear.parquet: Linear features of newly detected hedges.

  • disappeared_hedges_linear.parquet: Linear features of hedges no longer detected.

docs.title: Congratulations!
docs.text: You have completed the full haies pipeline, from raw data preprocessing to multi-temporal indicator analysis. For more technical details on the underlying modules, see the API Reference.

docs.title: Accelerating Training (Optional)
docs.text: Training the model on high-resolution rasters can be slow due to the overhead of reading and decoding many separate .jp2 files during each epoch. haies provides a CLI tool to convert these rasters into memory-mapped arrays (mmaps) for much faster training.

docs.title: Memory Mapping
docs.text: Memory mapping allows the trainer to access specific patches of data directly from disk as if they were in memory, without loading the entire dataset or opening many files. This is especially beneficial when working on HPC environments with large datasets.

docs.title: Running the Memory Mapping Tool
docs.text:
The haies_create_mmaps tool takes the results of the dataset split and preprocessed labels to create these binary arrays.

# Create memory-mapped arrays
haies_create_mmaps --config-path <path_to_config_dir> --config-name create_mmaps

docs.title: Configuration Details
docs.text:
The create_mmaps.yaml configuration defines the paths and settings for memory mapping:

Reference Configuration

The following configuration is used for memory mapping. You can also find the file at docs/example_configs/mmaps.yaml.

# conf/config.yaml
_target_: haies.clis.create_mmaps.Config

mmap_dir: ./data/mmaps
geometries_path: ./data/geometries.parquet
targets:
  - target_1
  - target_2
patch_size: null # Automatically inferred if null
resolution: 1.0
target_scaling_factor:
  target_1: 1.0
  # target_2 is not scaled
target_band_list:
  target_1: [0, 1, 2]
  # target_2 uses al bands
replace_nan_by_zero_in_input: false
replace_nan_by_zero_in_target: true
target_max_value:
  target_1: 100.0
  # target_2 has no max value
tiles_per_arr: 100

docs.title: Output Artifacts
docs.text: The tool will generate a set of .dat or .npy (depending on the backend) files in your mmap_dir. These files are much faster to read than the original rasters.

docs.title: Training with Mmaps
docs.text:
When you proceed to training, you can configure the data module to use the memory-mapped backend instead of the raster backend. This is done by setting the class_path in your training configuration:

data:
  init_args:
    backend_cfg:
      class_path: haies.data.config.FromMmapBackend
      init_args:
        mmap_dir: /path/to/save/mmaps
        tiles_per_arr: 100

Next Step: Model Training

docs.title: Running Predictions
docs.text: Once you have a trained model, the last step is to use it to generate hedge maps and canopy height models (CHM) on a new Area of Interest (AOI).

docs.title: Prediction Subcommand
docs.text:
The haies predict subcommand (via haies_predict or the general CLI) is used for inference.

# Run predictions over an AOI
haies predict \
    --config configs/reproducer/base.yaml \
    --config configs/reproducer/predict.yaml \
    --model.init_args.net_ckpt_path="/path/to/final/checkpoint/last.ckpt"

docs.title: Configuration Details
docs.text:
The predict.yaml configuration defines the paths for predictions and where to save the outputs:

Reference Configuration

The following configuration is used for running predictions. You can also find the file at docs/example_configs/reproducer/predict.yaml.

data:
  class_path: haies.data.MultiHeadDataModule
  init_args:
    cfg:
      predict_patch_size: 1024
      predict_batch_size: 1
      num_workers: 8
    backend_cfg:
      class_path: haies.data.config.FromRasterBackend
trainer:
  callbacks+:
    - class_path: haies.callbacks.PredictionsToTifs
      init_args:
        output_path: ./outputs/predictions
        targets:
          - height
          - segment
        merge_params:
          height:
            dtype: uint16
            normalization: 100.0
          segment:
            dtype: uint8
            normalization: 100.0
        async_merge: false

docs.title: Parallel Execution
docs.text:
Generating predictions for a large AOI can be time-consuming. You can parallelize the process across multiple GPUs or SLURM jobs by using different predict_glob_pattern settings for each instance.

For example, to process only tiles starting with 17-2024-0440:

haies predict \
    --config configs/reproducer/base.yaml \
    --config configs/reproducer/predict.yaml \
    --data.init_args.cfg.predict_glob_pattern="17-2024-0440-*" \
    --model.init_args.net_ckpt_path="/path/to/final/checkpoint/last.ckpt"

docs.title: Post-Processing
docs.text:
The output of haies predict will be a set of GeoTIFF files:

  1. Hedge Segmentation: A score map (0 to 100) for each pixel.

  2. Canopy Height Model: Predicted height (in centimeter) for each pixel.

Next Step: Computing Indicators

docs.title: Label Preprocessing
docs.text: The haies model is trained on soft segmentation labels derived from existing BD Haie and LiDAR HD from IGN, and on height directly. Before training, we must compute the segmentation labels.

docs.title: Running the Preprocessing
docs.text:
The haies_segment_hedges tool generates these labels.

# to use the config ./path/to/config/dir/preprocessing.yaml
haies_segment_hedges --config-path ./path/to/config/dir --config-name preprocessing

docs.title: Configuration Details
docs.text:
The tool expects a configuration (e.g., hedges.yaml) with the following structure:

Reference Configuration

The following configuration is used for label preprocessing. You can also find the file at docs/example_configs/preprocessing.yaml.

hedges_db_path: ./data/hedges.parquet
lidar_dir: ./data/lidar
# You can also provide a list of directories:
# lidar_dir:
#   - /path/to/dir1
#   - /path/to/dir2
save_dir: ./outputs/labels
dilation_edge: 5
min_height: 0.7
override: false

hydra:
  output_subdir: null

docs.title: Output Artifacts
docs.text:
The tool creates a directory containing the labels and the configuration used to generate them:

segmentation_labels/
segmentation_labels/segment_hedges_config.yaml
segmentation_labels/hedges_LHD_FXX_0458_6536_MNH_O_0M50_LAMB93_IGN69.jp2
segmentation_labels/hedges_LHD_FXX_0498_6517_MNH_O_0M50_LAMB93_IGN69.jp2
...

The labels are saved as .jp2 files (JPEG 2000), which are efficient for high-resolution spatial data.

Next Step: Dataset Splitting

docs.title: Environment Setup
docs.text: Before you begin using haies, ensure you have a correctly configured environment.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
The haies package can be installed directly from its repository. We recommend using a fresh virtual environment (e.g., using conda or venv) to avoid dependency conflicts.

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/LSCE-forest/haies.git
cd haies

# Install the package in editable mode with development dependencies
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: HPC Environments (e.g., Jean Zay)
docs.text:
If you're working on a high-performance cluster like Jean Zay, ensure you have the necessary environment modules loaded. You may want to use a SLURM-aware environment or a custom conda environment.

For GPU acceleration, verify your CUDA setup: 

python -c "import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available())"

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
A typical haies project expects the following directories:

  • data/: Raw input data (LiDAR, Orthophotos, Hedge databases).

  • outputs/: Where preprocessed labels, splits, and training checkpoints will be stored.

  • configs/: YAML configuration files for each stage of the pipeline.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Once installed, verify the installation by running the help command for the main CLI tool:

haies --help

You should see a list of available subcommands like fit, predict, and validate.

Next Step: Data Download

docs.title: Dataset Splitting
docs.text: With the labels preprocessed, the next step is to divide your Area of Interest (AOI) into training, validation, and test sets. This ensures unbiased evaluation and prevents data leakage.

docs.title: Spatial Splitting
docs.text: haies uses a spatial split strategy. It divides the AOI into non-overlapping tiles, ensuring that training and evaluation data are geographically separated. A buffer zone is often used between sets to prevent spatial autocorrelation.

docs.title: Running the Splitting Tool
docs.text:
The haies_create_split tool automates this process.

# Create the dataset split
haies_create_split --config-path <path_to_config_dir> --config-name create_split

docs.title: Configuration Details
docs.text:
The create_split.yaml configuration defines the parameters for the split:

Reference Configuration

The following configuration is used for dataset splitting. You can also find the file at docs/example_configs/splitting.yaml.

# conf/config.yaml
_target_: haies.clis.create_split.Config

version: null  # Automatically set to timestamp if null

save_dir: ./outputs/splits
targets_data_dir:
  target_1: [./data/targets_1]
  target_2: [./data/targets_2]
bdortho_dir: [./data/bdortho]

test_side_length: 5_000
buffer: 1_000
test_area: 100_000_000
val_proportion: 0.1

seed: 42
overwrite: true
convert_to_tif: false

docs.title: Output Artifacts
docs.text:
The tool generates several important files in your save_dir:

  1. geometries.parquet: Contains the boundaries and set assignment (train/val/test) for each tile.

  2. create_splits_config.yaml: The configuration used for the split.

  3. splits.pdf: A visualization of the resulting split, such as the following

docs.title: Class Imbalance and Loss Weighting
docs.text:
Hedges typically occupy only a small fraction of the landscape. To address this class imbalance, you need to calculate the ratio of hedge pixels in your training set.

Using gdalinfo -stats on the generated labels or analyzing the geometries.parquet file, you can determine the observed mean \(\mu\) of your labels. For a BCEWithLogitsLoss, the positive weight should be:

\[pos\_weight = \frac{1}{\mu} - 1\]

This value will be used in the Model Training configuration.

docs.title: Accelerating Training (Optional)
docs.text:
haies_create_split defines the sets, but it does not create the data for training. If you want to accelerate training, you can convert the rasters into memory-mapped arrays using Memory Mapping. Otherwise, you can proceed directly to Model Training.

Next Step: Accelerating Training (Optional) or Model Training

docs.title: Model Training
docs.text: The haies model is a multi-head deep learning architecture (e.g., based on UNet or PVTv2) that simultaneously performs hedge segmentation and canopy height estimation.

docs.title: Training Strategy
docs.text:
Training is divided into two phases for optimal stability:

  1. Phase 1: Pretraining: Training on height estimation only. This allows the model to learn the basic spatial structures and textures of the landscape before tackling the more challenging task of hedge segmentation.

  2. Phase 2: Full Training: Fine-tuning the model for both heights and hedge segmentation, starting from the weights learned in Phase 1.

docs.title: Training Subcommand
docs.text:
The haies fit subcommand is used for both training phases.

# General training command
haies fit \
    --config configs/reproducer/base.yaml \
    --config configs/reproducer/train.yaml \
    --model.init_args.net_ckpt_path="/path/to/checkpoints/last.ckpt"

docs.title: Configuration Files
docs.text:
haies uses multiple YAML files to define the training environment.

Reference Configurations

Reference configurations for training are shown below. You can also find them in docs/example_configs/reproducer/.

base.yamlpretrain.yamltrain.yaml 
seed_everything: 41 # replace with true for a random seed
data:
  class_path: haies.data.MultiHeadDataModule
  init_args:
    cfg:
      targets: [height, segment]
      geometries_path: ./data/geometries.parquet
      target_scaling_factor:
        height: 0.157 # lidarhd has been mapped from [0, 40] to [0, 255]. We map it back to [0, 40].
        # segment has no scaling factor
      resolution: 0.2
      normalization_constants:
        mean: [110, 110, 100]
        std: [45, 40, 40]
      target_max_value:
        height: 40
      patch_size: 512
      batch_size: 8
      num_workers: 8
      persistent_workers: true
      replace_nan_by_zero_in_target: false
      replace_nan_by_zero_in_input: false
    backend_cfg:
      class_path: haies.data.config.FeatureFromMmapTargetsFromRasterBackend
      init_args:
        mmap_dir: ./data/mmaps
        tiles_per_arr: 100
model:
  class_path: haies.modules.multihead_module.MultiHeadModule
  init_args:
    mask_threshold: 0.5
    activation_pred: # activation for each head (for predictions only, as activation is usually integrated in the loss for training)
      segment:
        class_path: torch.nn.Sigmoid
    target_types:
      height: regression
      segment: segmentation
    net:
      class_path: haies.nets.unet.UNet
      init_args:
        n_channels: 3
        n_heads: 1
    criterion:
      class_path: haies.losses.multihead_loss.MultiHeadLoss
      init_args:
        loss_functions:
          height:
            class_path: torch.nn.HuberLoss
            # class_path: torch.nn.MSELoss
          segment:
            class_path: torch.nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss
            init_args:
              pos_weight:
                class_path: haies.utils.utils.tensor_factory
                init_args:
                  data: [60]
                  dtype: float32
        pooling_size: 1
    optimizer:
      class_path: torch.optim.AdamW
      init_args:
        lr: &lr 1e-5
        weight_decay: 1e-5
    lr_scheduler:
      class_path: sprout.utils.torch.OneAfterAnotherSchedulerCLI
      init_args:
        schedulers:
          - class_path: torch.optim.lr_scheduler.OneCycleLR
            init_args:
              max_lr: 1e-4
              total_steps: &n_warmup_epochs 50
          - class_path: sprout.utils.torch.SetLR
            init_args:
              lr: *lr
          - class_path: torch.optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR
            init_args:
              T_max: 100
              eta_min: 5e-7
        nsteps: [*n_warmup_epochs, 1, -1]
trainer:
  strategy: auto
  # strategy: ddp_find_unused_parameters_true
  default_root_dir: &root_dir_trainer ./outputs/training
  accelerator: auto
  devices: 1
  num_nodes: 1
  accumulate_grad_batches: 4
  deterministic: True
  max_epochs: 150
  precision: 16
  plugins:
    - class_path: lightning.pytorch.plugins.environments.SLURMEnvironment
      init_args:
        requeue_signal: SIGUSR1
  logger:
    class_path: lightning.pytorch.loggers.TensorBoardLogger
    init_args:
      save_dir: *root_dir_trainer
      name: alpha
      version: null
  log_every_n_steps: 10
  enable_progress_bar: True
  max_time: null
  check_val_every_n_epoch: 1
ckpt_path: null

data:
  class_path: haies.data.MultiHeadDataModule
  init_args:
    cfg:
      targets: [height]
model:
  class_path: haies.modules.multihead_module.MultiHeadModule
  init_args:
    activation_pred:
    target_types:
      height: regression
    net:
      init_args:
        # num_classes: 1
        n_heads: 1
    criterion:
      class_path: haies.losses.multihead_loss.MultiHeadLoss
      init_args:
        loss_functions:
          height:
            class_path: torch.nn.HuberLoss
        pooling_size: 1
trainer:
  callbacks:
    - class_path: lightning.pytorch.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint
      init_args:
        save_top_k: 3
        save_last: link
        monitor: val/epoch_loss
        save_weights_only: False # Keep this option to False in order to be able to resume training from checkpoint
        mode: min
    - class_path: lightning.pytorch.callbacks.LearningRateMonitor
      init_args:
        logging_interval: epoch
    - class_path: haies.callbacks.GeoImageTensorboardLogger
      init_args:
        pred_key: height
        every_n_epochs: 20
        viz_params:
          Feature:
            vmin: -1.2
            vmax: 1.2
          Label:
            vmax: 40
          Prediction:
            vmax: 40
    - class_path: haies.callbacks.LogErrorScatterPlot
      init_args:
        target: height
        figdpi: 40
        range_fig: [-1, 30]
        gridsize: 127
        min_label_value: 0

trainer:
  callbacks:
    - class_path: lightning.pytorch.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint # Save the best checkpoint based on the maximum val_acc recorded.
      init_args:
        save_top_k: 3
        save_last: link
        monitor: val/epoch_loss
        save_weights_only: False # Keep this option to False in order to be able to resume training from checkpoint
        mode: min
    - class_path: lightning.pytorch.callbacks.LearningRateMonitor
      init_args:
        logging_interval: epoch
    - class_path: haies.callbacks.GeoImageTensorboardLogger
      init_args:
        pred_key: height
        every_n_epochs: 20
        viz_params:
          Feature:
            vmin: -1.2
            vmax: 1.2
          Label:
            vmax: 40
          Prediction:
            vmax: 40
    - class_path: haies.callbacks.GeoImageTensorboardLogger
      init_args:
        pred_key: segment
        viz_head:
          class_path: torch.nn.Sigmoid
        every_n_epochs: 20
        viz_params:
          Feature:
            vmin: -1.2
            vmax: 1.2
          Label:
            vmin: 0
            vmax: 1
          Prediction:
            vmax: 1
    - class_path: haies.callbacks.LogErrorScatterPlot
      init_args:
        target: height
        figdpi: 40
        range_fig: [2, 30]
        gridsize: 60
        min_label_value: 0
    - class_path: haies.callbacks.LogErrorScatterPlot
      init_args:
        target: segment
        figdpi: 40
        range_fig: [0, 1]
        gridsize: 20
        min_label_value: 0

docs.title: Phase 1: Pretraining
docs.text:
Train the model on the height target only.

haies fit \
    --config configs/reproducer/base.yaml \
    --config configs/reproducer/pretrain.yaml

docs.title: Phase 2: Full Training
docs.text:
After pretraining, train the full model. Ensure you provide the path to the best checkpoint from Phase 1 using --model.init_args.net_ckpt_path.

haies fit \
    --config configs/reproducer/base.yaml \
    --config configs/reproducer/train.yaml \
    --model.init_args.net_ckpt_path="/path/to/pretrain/checkpoints/last.ckpt"

docs.title: Monitoring Training
docs.text:
Progress and metrics are logged automatically. You can monitor them using TensorBoard:

tensorboard --logdir /path/to/trainer/logs

Look for the following metrics:

  • val/height/huber: Height estimation error (lower is better).

  • val/segment/f1: Segmentation quality (higher is better).

Next Step: Running Predictions
